title: EXPOSÉ XXII A CONGRUENCE FORMULA FOR THE ζ FUNCTION

pages.title: EXPOSÉ XXII A CONGRUENCE FORMULA FOR THE ζ FUNCTION

blocks.html:
EXPOSÉ XXII
A CONGRUENCE FORMULA FOR THE ζ FUNCTIONby N. KATZ

Let \(X\) be a proper scheme over \(\mathbb F_q\). Its zeta function, considered as an element of \(\mathbb Z[[T]]\), can be reduced modulo \(p\); we wish to compute it modulo \(p\). One finds an expression of the type one could have expected ever since the fixed point formulas of « Woods Hole » type, as an alternating product of characteristic polynomials of a suitable « Frobenius » acting on any one of the following three cohomology spaces:

1. 1) the coherent cohomology \(H^i(X,\mathcal O_X)\) (3.1.1)
  2. 2) the De Rham cohomology \(H^i_{\mathrm{DR}}(X)=\mathbb H^i(X,\Omega_X^\bullet)\) (3.1.1)
  3. 3) the étale cohomology \(H^i_{\mathrm{et}}(X,\mathbb Z/p\mathbb Z)\) (3.1.2).

This expression should be seen as an improvement of a special case of the Woods Hole trace formula: that of the structure sheaf and the Frobenius endomorphism. This case is, in fact, a corollary of the congruence formula (cf. 3.2.1).
The method of this exposé is completely naive: it consists in reducing to the case of a hypersurface, then in « reducing modulo \(p\) »

the computation by DWORK of the true zeta function of a hypersurface. By « reducing » carefully, one finds the improvement 5.0.6 due to AX [1] (and also to DWORK in the smooth case [4, § 7]) of the well-known theorem of WARNING-CHEVALLEY [8].

Another method, entirely cohomological and in the spirit of SGA 5, applying also to « twisted coefficients », has just been found by DELIGNE, and should appear in the re-edition of SGA 5; it rests on the trace formula of « Woods Hole » type, and the « symmetric Künneth formula » of DELIGNE (SGA 4 XVII 5.5.21).
1.0. Let \(V\) be a finite-dimensional vector space over a field \(k\) of characteristic \(p>0\). Denote by \(q\) a power of \(p\). A map
is called q-linear if it is additive and if it satisfies

1.0.3. One should pay attention to the fact that the \(n\)-th iterate \(\varphi^n\) of a \(q\)-linear endomorphism is \(q^n\)-linear, and that, if \(k\) is not perfect, the image \(\varphi(V)\) of a \(q\)-linear endomorphism is not necessarily a \(k\)-vector subspace.
1.0.4. For each extension \(K/k\), a \(q\)-linear endomorphism \(\varphi\) of \(V\) gives rise to a unique \(q\)-linear endomorphism \(\varphi_K\) of \(V_K=V\otimes_kK\), satisfying the formula
1.0.6. We say that a \(q\)-linear endomorphism \(\varphi\) of \(V\) is semi-simple if its image generates \(V\) as a \(k\)-vector space, and that it is nilpotent if it has a vanishing iterate.
Evidently, \(\varphi\) restricted to \(V_{\mathrm{ss}}\) is semi-simple, and \(\varphi\) restricted to \(V_{\mathrm{nilp}}\) is nilpotent. If \(k\) is perfect, we have
but, if \(k\) is not perfect, (1.0.9) becomes inexact, for there are \(q\)-linear endomorphisms which are injective without being semi-simple.

Proposition 1.1.

Let \(\varphi\) be a \(q\)-linear endomorphism of a finite-dimensional vector space \(V\) over a separably closed field \(k\). If \(\varphi\) is semi-simple then, setting

\[ V^{1-\varphi}=\text{the }\mathbb F_q\text{-vector space of fixed points of }\varphi =\operatorname{Ker}(1-\varphi), \]

we have

\[ \tag{1.1.1}V\xleftarrow{\sim}(V^{1-\varphi})\otimes_{\mathbb F_q}k. \]

Corollary 1.1.2.
\[ V^{1-\varphi^n}\xleftarrow{\sim}(V^{1-\varphi})\otimes_{\mathbb F_q}\mathbb F_{q^n}. \]
Let \(e\) be a \(k\)-basis of \(V\), and let us write
the arrow « raising the coordinates to the \(q\)-th power ». Then for \(g\in\operatorname{GL}(n,k)\), \(ge\) is a basis of \(V\) formed of fixed points of \(\varphi\) if and only if
Denote by \(\bar k\) an algebraic closure of \(k\). The group \(\operatorname{GL}(n,\bar k)\) acts on the variety \(\operatorname{GL}(n,\bar k)\) by
is étale, as one sees by computing its tangent map, hence the orbits are open. Since \(\operatorname{GL}(n,\bar k)\) is irreducible, there is only one orbit; in other words, the arrow
is surjective. Because it is also étale, it follows, \(k\) being separably closed, that the arrow

is surjective, which means that \(V\) admits a \(k\)-basis formed of fixed points of \(\varphi\). It is then clear, \(\varphi\) being \(q\)-linear, that every fixed point of \(\varphi\) lies in the \(\mathbb F_q\)-vector space generated by a \(k\)-basis formed of the fixed points of \(\varphi\), which proves 1.1.

Corollary 1.1.10.

Let \(\varphi\) be a \(q\)-linear endomorphism of a finite-dimensional vector space \(V\) over a separably closed field \(k\). Then we have a canonical isomorphism

\[ \tag{1.1.11}V_{\mathrm{ss}}\xleftarrow{\sim} V^{1-\varphi}\otimes_{\mathbb F_q}k. \]

Proposition 1.2.

Let \(\varphi\) be a \(q\)-linear endomorphism of a finite-dimensional vector space over a separably closed field \(k\). Then the additive map

\[ 1-\varphi:V\longrightarrow V \]

is surjective.
Proof. We have an exact sequence of abelian groups

on which \(1-\varphi\) acts. By the « snake lemma », it suffices to treat \(V_{\mathrm{ss}}\), which is clear by 1.1 using a basis formed of the fixed points, and \(V/V_{\mathrm{ss}}\), on which \(\varphi\) is nilpotent, hence on which \(1-\varphi\) is invertible.

Proposition 2.0.

Let \(\bar X\) be a proper scheme over a separably closed field \(k\), and denote by

\[ \tag{2.0.1}\overline{\mathcal F}_p:H^i(\bar X,\mathcal O_{\bar X})\longrightarrow H^i(\bar X,\mathcal O_{\bar X}) \]

the \(p\)-linear map induced by

\[ \tag{2.0.2}\overline{\mathcal F}_p:\mathcal O_{\bar X}\longrightarrow\mathcal O_{\bar X}, \qquad \overline{\mathcal F}_p(f)=f^p. \]

Denote by \(H^i_{\mathrm{et}}(\bar X,\mathbb Z/p\mathbb Z)\) the étale cohomology groups with coefficients in the constant sheaf \(\mathbb Z/p\mathbb Z\). Then we have canonical isomorphisms:

\[ \tag{2.0.3}H^i_{\mathrm{et}}(\bar X,\mathbb Z/p\mathbb Z)\simeq (H^i(\bar X,\mathcal O_{\bar X}))^{1-\overline{\mathcal F}_p}. \]
Proof. We have an exact sequence of sheaves on \(\bar X_{\mathrm{et}}\)
\[ \tag{2.0.4}0\longrightarrow\mathbb Z/p\mathbb Z\longrightarrow \mathcal O_{\bar X}\xrightarrow{1-\overline{\mathcal F}_p}\mathcal O_{\bar X} \longrightarrow0 \]

\[ \tag{2.0.5}\cdots\longrightarrow H^i_{\mathrm{et}}(\bar X,\mathbb Z/p\mathbb Z) \longrightarrow H^i_{\mathrm{et}}(\bar X,\mathcal O_{\bar X}) \xrightarrow{1-\overline{\mathcal F}_p}H^i_{\mathrm{et}}(\bar X,\mathcal O_{\bar X}) \longrightarrow\cdots. \]

We have \(H^i_{\mathrm{et}}(\bar X,\mathcal O_{\bar X})\simeq H^i(\bar X,\mathcal O_{\bar X})\); it is a finite-dimensional \(k\)-vector space, \(\bar X\) being proper. Applying 1.2, we find exact sequences

\[ \tag{2.0.6}0\longrightarrow H^i_{\mathrm{et}}(\bar X,\mathbb Z/p\mathbb Z) \longrightarrow H^i(\bar X,\mathcal O_{\bar X}) \xrightarrow{1-\overline{\mathcal F}_p}H^i(\bar X,\mathcal O_{\bar X}) \longrightarrow0, \]

Corollary 2.1.

For \(\bar X\) proper over a separably closed field \(k\),

\[ \tag{2.1.1}H^i(\bar X,\mathcal O_{\bar X})_{\mathrm{ss}} \xleftarrow{\sim}H^i_{\mathrm{et}}(\bar X,\mathbb Z/p\mathbb Z) \otimes_{\mathbb F_p}k. \]
2.2. Let now \(f:X\longrightarrow\operatorname{Spec}(\mathbb F_q)\) be a proper scheme over \(\mathbb F_q\). Denote by \(k\) an algebraic closure of \(\mathbb F_q\), and set

The étale sheaf \(R^if_*(\mathbb Z/p\mathbb Z)\) on \(\operatorname{Sp}(\mathbb F_q)\) is identified with \(H^i(\bar X,\mathbb Z/p\mathbb Z)\), viewed as a \(\operatorname{Gal}(k/\mathbb F_q)\) module. Denote by
the linear operation (it is \(q\)-linear, but we are over \(\mathbb F_q\)!) induced by

Proposition 2.2.4.

Denote by \(\varphi_q\in\operatorname{Gal}(k/\mathbb F_q)\) the « Frobenius » element, \(\varphi_q(\lambda)=\lambda^q\) and let \(i\in\mathbb Z\). We have an identity of characteristic polynomials

\[ \tag{2.2.4.1}\det_{\mathbb F_p}\!\left(1-t\varphi_q^{-1}\mid H^i_{\mathrm{et}}(\bar X,\mathbb Z/p\mathbb Z)\right) =\det_{\mathbb F_q}\!\left(1-t\mathcal F_q\mid H^i(X,\mathcal O_X)\right). \]

where the first member is the characteristic polynomial of the element \(\varphi_q^{-1}\) in \(\pi_1(\operatorname{Sp}(\mathbb F_q))\) acting on the Galois module \(H^i(\bar X,\mathbb Z/p\mathbb Z)\).

Proposition 2.2.5.

Let \(X\) be proper over \(\operatorname{Spec}(\mathbb F_q)\). There is a canonical isomorphism

\[ \tag{2.2.6}H^i(X,\mathcal O_X)_{\mathrm{ss}}\otimes_{\mathbb F_q}k \xleftarrow{\sim}H^i_{\mathrm{et}}(\bar X,\mathbb Z/p\mathbb Z) \otimes_{\mathbb F_p}k \]

which transforms the automorphism \(\mathcal F_q\otimes_{\mathbb F_q}k\) of the first member into the automorphism \(\varphi_q^{-1}\otimes_{\mathbb F_p}k\) of the second (which can also be interpreted as the endomorphism \(\operatorname{Fr}_q^{X*}\otimes_{\mathbb F_p}k\), where \(\operatorname{fr}_q^X:\bar X\longrightarrow\bar X\) is the \(k\)-endomorphism of Frobenius. cf. SGA 5, XVIII, 2.3.4).
\[ \tag{2.2.7}H^i(\bar X,\mathcal O_{\bar X})\xleftarrow{\sim} H^i(X,\mathcal O_X)\otimes_{\mathbb F_q}k, \]
so that we obtain the isomorphism 2.2.6 by composing the isomorphisms 2.2.8 and 2.1.1.
\[ \tag{2.2.10}\widetilde{\mathcal F}_q:H^i(\bar X,\mathcal O_{\bar X}) \longrightarrow H^i(\bar X,\mathcal O_{\bar X}), \]
and also admits a \(k\)-linear extension, still denoted \(\mathcal F_q\)
\[ \tag{2.2.11}\mathcal F_q:H^i(\bar X,\mathcal O_{\bar X}) \longrightarrow H^i(\bar X,\mathcal O_{\bar X}). \]
\[ \tag{2.2.12}\varphi_q:H^i(\bar X,\mathcal O_{\bar X}) \longrightarrow H^i(\bar X,\mathcal O_{\bar X}) \]

the \(q\)-linear operation whose fixed points are \(H^i(X,\mathcal O_X)\). The operators \(\mathcal F_q,\varphi_q\) and \(\widetilde{\mathcal F}_q\), by means of (2.2.7), can also be written \(\mathcal F_q\otimes\operatorname{id}_k\), \(\operatorname{id}\otimes\varphi_q\), \(\mathcal F_q\otimes\varphi_q\), hence commute with one another, and we have

and they all commute with \(\widetilde{\mathcal F}_p\), so that \(\mathcal F_q\) and \(\varphi_q\) act on \(H^i(\bar X,\mathcal O_{\bar X})^{1-\widetilde{\mathcal F}_q}\) and on \(H^i(\bar X,\mathcal O_{\bar X})^{1-\widetilde{\mathcal F}_p}\). Now, \(\mathcal F_q\) and \(\varphi_q\) are inverse to each other on \(H^i(\bar X,\mathcal O_{\bar X})^{1-\widetilde{\mathcal F}_q}\), by (2.2.13). Hence the operation of \(\mathcal F_q\) on
\[ H^i(\bar X,\mathcal O_{\bar X})_{\mathrm{ss}} \xleftarrow{\sim} H^i(\bar X,\mathcal O_{\bar X})^{1-\widetilde{\mathcal F}_q} \otimes_{\mathbb F_q}k\qquad\text{(cf. 1.1.11)} \]
is the inverse of the operation of \(\varphi_q\otimes k\) on
\[ H^i(\bar X,\mathcal O_{\bar X})^{1-\widetilde{\mathcal F}_q} \otimes_{\mathbb F_q}k. \]
\[ \tag{2.2.14}H^i(\bar X,\mathcal O_{\bar X})^{1-\widetilde{\mathcal F}_q} \xleftarrow{\sim} H^i(\bar X,\mathcal O_{\bar X})^{1-\widetilde{\mathcal F}_p} \otimes_{\mathbb F_p}\mathbb F_q, \]

the automorphism \(\varphi_q\) of \(H^i(\bar X,\mathcal O_{\bar X})^{1-\widetilde{\mathcal F}_q}\) becomes, via 2.2.14, the automorphism \(\varphi_q\otimes F_q\) of \(H^i(\bar X,\mathcal O_{\bar X})^{1-\widetilde{\mathcal F}_p} \otimes_{\mathbb F_p}\mathbb F_q\), so that the automorphism \(\varphi_q\otimes k\) of \(H^i(\bar X,\mathcal O_{\bar X})_{\mathrm{ss}}\) becomes, via 2.1.1, the automorphism \(\varphi_q\otimes k\) of \(H^i_{\mathrm{et}}(\bar X,\mathbb Z/p\mathbb Z)\otimes_{\mathbb F_p}k\), which completes the proof of (2.2.5).

pages.title: 3. The congruence formula: statements and elementary equivalences

blocks.html: 3. The congruence formula: statements and elementary equivalences
I wish to thank Grothendieck and Deligne for having explained to me the variants (6.1.2), (3.1.1.2) and (3.1.3) of (3.1.1).

3.0. Let \(X\) be a scheme of finite type over \(\mathbb F_q\). We define the degree of a closed point \(y\in X\) as the degree of its residue field \(\mathbb F_q(y)/\mathbb F_q\). We define the zeta function of \(X/\mathbb F_q\), as an element of \(\mathbb Z[[t]]\), by the formula (cf. also SGA 5 XIII 3.1)
By the « zeta function modulo \(p\) » we always mean the image of \(Z(t,X/\mathbb F_q)\) in \(\mathbb F_q[[t]]\) under the « reduction modulo \(p\mathbb Z[[t]]\) » arrow

Theorem 3.1.

Let \(X\) be a proper scheme over \(\mathbb F_q\). Then

\[ \tag{3.1.1}Z(t,X/\mathbb F_q)\equiv \prod_{i\geq0}\det\left(1-t\mathcal F_q\mid H^i(X,\mathcal O_X)\right)^{(-1)^{i+1}} \pmod p \]

or, what is equivalent by (2.2.4),

\[ \tag{3.1.2}Z(t,X/\mathbb F_q)\equiv \prod_{i\geq0}\det\left(1-t\varphi_q^{-1}\mid H^i_{\mathrm{et}}(\bar X,\mathbb Z/p\mathbb Z)\right)^{(-1)^{i+1}} \pmod p. \]

Corollary 3.2.

Let \(X\) be a proper scheme over \(\mathbb F_q\). Then

\[ \tag{3.2.1}\sum_{i\geq0}(-1)^i\operatorname{trace} (\mathcal F_q\mid H^i(X,\mathcal O_X))\equiv\#X(\mathbb F_q)\pmod p \]

or, what is equivalent by (2.2.4)

\[ \tag{3.2.2}\sum_{i\geq0}(-1)^i\operatorname{tr} (\varphi_q^{-1}\mid H^i_{\mathrm{et}}(\bar X,\mathbb Z/p\mathbb Z)) \equiv\#X(\mathbb F_q)\pmod p. \]
One takes the coefficients of \(t\) in the congruences (3.1.1) and (3.1.2).

Theorem 3.3.

Let \(X\) be a proper scheme over \(\mathbb F_q\); we denote by \(H^i_{\mathrm{DR}}(X/\mathbb F_q)\) the \(\mathbb F_q\)-vector spaces \(H^i(X,\Omega^\bullet_{X/\mathbb F_q})\), on which \(\mathcal F_q\) acts. We have

\[ \tag{3.3.1}Z(t,X/\mathbb F_q)\equiv \prod_{i\geq0}\det(1-t\mathcal F_q\mid H^i_{\mathrm{DR}}(X/\mathbb F_q))^{(-1)^{i+1}} \pmod p. \]

on which \(\mathcal F_q\) acts (via the endomorphism \(\mathcal F_p=\operatorname{Fr}_p^{X*}\) deduced from \(\operatorname{fr}_q^X:X\longrightarrow X\)) by killing \(E_1^{p,q}\) for \(q\neq0\), one sees that (3.3.1) is equivalent to (3.1.1).
The formation of the « semi-simple part » (cf. 1.0.7) being an exact functor, one deduces from (3.3.2) a spectral sequence
Since \((E_1^{p,q})_{\mathrm{ss}}=0\) for \(q\neq0\), one deduces from it for every \(i\) an isomorphism
\[ \tag{3.3.5}\det(1-t\mathcal F_q\mid H^i_{\mathrm{DR}}(X/\mathbb F_q)) =\det(1-t\mathcal F_q\mid H^i(X,\mathcal O_X)). \]

Theorem 3.4.

Let \(X\) be a separated scheme of finite type over \(\mathbb F_q\). Then, denoting by \(H_c^i\) the étale cohomology with proper supports (SGA 4 XVII 5.1.9 (iii)), we have

\[ \tag{3.4.1}Z(t,X/\mathbb F_q)\equiv \prod_{i\geq0}\det(1-t\varphi_q^{-1}\mid H_c^i(\bar X,\mathbb Z/p\mathbb Z))^{(-1)^{i+1}}\pmod p. \]
Let us begin by proving the equivalence of (3.4.1) and (3.1.1).
\[ \tag{3.4.2}Z(X)\quad\text{instead of }Z(t,X/\mathbb F_q) \] \[ \tag{3.4.3}Z'(X)\quad\text{instead of } \prod_i\det(1-t\varphi_q^{-1}\mid H_c^i(\bar X,\mathbb Z/p\mathbb Z))^{(-1)^{i+1}}. \]

Lemma 3.5.

If \(\{U_i\}\) is a finite covering of \(X\) by open subschemes we have

\[ \tag{3.5.1}Z'(X)=\prod_{j\geq0}\prod_{1\leq i_0<\cdots<i_j\leq n} Z'(U_{i_0}\cap\cdots\cap U_{i_j})^{(-1)^j}. \]
Proof. Follows easily from the localization spectral sequence (SGA 4 XVII 6.2.10)
\[ E_1^{a,b}=\bigoplus_{i_0<\cdots<i_b} H_c^a(\bar U_{i_0}\cap\cdots\cap\bar U_{i_b},\mathbb Z/p\mathbb Z) \Longrightarrow H_c^{a+b}(\bar X,\mathbb Z/p\mathbb Z). \]
One can also obtain it by induction on \(n\), applying 3.6 below.

Lemma 3.6.

If \(U\) is an open subset of \(X\), \(Y\) the closed set \(X-U\),

\[ \tag{3.6.1}Z'(X)=Z'(U)Z'(Y). \]
Proof. Follows from the exact cohomology sequence
\[ \tag{3.6.2}\longrightarrow H_c^i(\bar U,\mathbb Z/p\mathbb Z) \longrightarrow H_c^i(\bar X,\mathbb Z/p\mathbb Z) \longrightarrow H_c^i(\bar Y,\mathbb Z/p\mathbb Z)\longrightarrow. \]

Lemma 3.7 (Moebius).

Let \(B\) be a finite set, \(\{A_i\}\) a finite covering by subsets. Then

\[ \tag{3.7.1}\operatorname{card}(B)=\sum_{j\geq0}(-1)^j \sum_{i_0<\cdots<i_j}\operatorname{card}(A_{i_0}\cap\cdots\cap A_{i_j}). \]
Proof. We have the Leray spectral sequence for the rational cohomology of the discrete space \(B\) and the open covering \(\{A_i\}\)
and one computes the Euler-Poincaré characteristic of \(B\).

Lemma 3.8.

If \(\{U_i\}\) is a finite covering of \(X\) by subschemes, then

\[ \tag{3.8.1}Z(X)=\prod_{j\geq0}\prod_{i_0<\cdots<i_j} Z(U_{i_0}\cap\cdots\cap U_{i_j})^{(-1)^j}. \]

Lemma 3.9.

Suppose that the formula (3.4.1) (or, what amounts to the same thing, (3.1.2) i.e. (3.1.1)) is true for \(X\) a projective hypersurface. Then it is true for every \(X\) separated of finite type.

Proof. By 3.8 and 3.6, (3.4.1) is true for the complement of a hypersurface, \(\mathbb P^n-H\). Taking into account that \(\bigcap(\mathbb P^n-H_i)=\mathbb P^n-\bigcup H_i\) is again the complement of a hypersurface, the open covering \(\{\mathbb P^n-H_i\}\) of \(\mathbb P^n-\bigcap H_i\) allows us to apply 3.8 and 3.5 to deduce (3.1.3) for \(\mathbb P^n-\bigcap H_i\). By 3.8 and 3.6, (3.4.1) is true for \(\bigcap H_i\), i.e. for every projective \(X\). A further application of 3.8 and 3.5 gives 3.1.3 for every quasi-projective \(X\), in particular for every affine \(X\). Finally, for \(X\) separated of finite type, one takes a finite covering by affine open sets, and applies 3.8 and 3.5.

pages.title: 4. The computation of the zeta function of a hypersurface à la Dwork

blocks.html: 4. The computation of the zeta function of a hypersurface à la Dwork

whose vanishing defines the hypersurface \(V\subset\mathbb P^{n+1}\), and denote by \(V^*\) the open subset of \(V\) where \(\prod_{i=1}^{n+2}X_i\) is invertible, \(V^*_{\mathrm{aff}}\) the affine cone of \(V^*\).
Let us choose a nontrivial additive character
\(\mathbb C\) being a fixed algebraically closed field of characteristic zero (e.g. \(\mathbb C=\mathbb C\)), so that we have
Let us write explicitly the form \(f(X)\) as a sum of monomials
where \(w=(w_1,\ldots,w_{n+2})\) with \(\sum w_i=d\), \(X^w\mathrel{\mathop=\limits^{\mathrm{dfn}}}X_1^{w_1}\cdots X_{n+2}^{w_{n+2}}\), so that (4.0.7) can be rewritten
4.1. We must now make explicit our “additive character with values in characteristic zero”. We are tempted to take simply

\[ \tag{4.1.1}\Omega\quad\text{the algebraic closure of }\mathbb Q_p \] \[ \tag{4.1.2}\operatorname{ord}:\Omega^*\longrightarrow\mathbb Q \quad\text{the ordinal, normalized by }\operatorname{ord}(p)=1 \] \[ \tag{4.1.3}\zeta_p\in\Omega\quad\text{a primitive \(p\)-th root of unity.} \]
(NB. One sees at once that the left-hand side converges in \(\mathbb Q_p(\zeta_p)\).)
which, miraculously, lies in \(\mathbb Z_p[[X]]\). We define
whose fundamental properties are summarized in the following lemma:

Lemma 4.2.
\[ \tag{4.2.1}\Theta(X)=\sum B_nX^n,\qquad B_0=1,\quad A_1=\pi, \quad\operatorname{ord}(B_n)\geq\frac n{p-1}. \] \[ \tag{4.2.2}\Theta(1)=\zeta_p,\quad\text{a primitive \(p\)-th root of unity.} \]

(4.2.3) Let \(t\in\Omega\), and suppose that, for a given integer \(a>0\), \(t=t^{p^a}\) (i.e. \(t\) is a “Teichmüller representative” in \(W(\mathbb F_{q^a})\subset\Omega\)). Then \(\sum_{j=0}^{a-1}t^{p^j} \in\mathbb Z_p\), so that one can define the formal power series

\[ (\Theta(X))^{\sum_{j=0}^{a-1}t^{p^j}}, \]

and we have an identity of formal power series

\[ \tag{4.2.3.1}\prod_{j=0}^{a-1}\Theta(t^{p^j}X) =(\Theta(X))^{\sum_{j=0}^{a-1}t^{p^j}}. \]
Proof. Taking into account that \(E(X)\in\mathbb Z_p[[X]]\), (4.2.1) follows from the definition. As for (4.2.2), we have
By (4.2.4), there exists an integer \(n\geq0\) such that \(\Theta(1)^{p^n}=1\), (this is the characterization of the kernel of the logarithm) and (4.2.5) implies that \(n=1\).

As for (4.2.3.1), taking logarithms, it follows from an obvious computation, once it is proved that one indeed has \(\sum_{j=0}^{a-1}t^{p^j}\in\mathbb Z_p\). Now one sees at once that the \(t^{p^j}\) \((0\leq j\leq a-1)\) are the conjugates of \(t\in W(\mathbb F_{q^a})\) over \(\mathbb Z_p\), hence
Taking the value at \(X=1\) of both sides of (4.2.3.1) we find the

Corollary 4.3.

Let \(t\in\Omega\) satisfy \(t^{p^a}=t\). Denote by \(\bar t\in\mathbb F_{p^a}\) its residue class. Then we have

\[ \tag{4.3.1}\prod_{j=0}^{a-1}\Theta(t^{p^j}) =\zeta_p^{\operatorname{tr}_{\mathbb F_{p^a}/\mathbb F_p}(\bar t)}. \]
4.4. Recall that the residue field of the ring of integers of \(\mathbb Q_p(\zeta_p)\) is \(\mathbb F_p\). Let

\[ \tag{4.4.0}K=\text{the unramified extension of }\mathbb Q_p(\zeta_p)\text{ with residue field }\mathbb F_q, \] \[ \tag{4.4.1}\tau\in\operatorname{Gal}(K/\mathbb Q_p(\zeta_p))\text{ the « Frobenius », i.e. the automorphism} \]
reducing according to the Frobenius automorphism of \(\mathbb F_q/\mathbb F_p\), \(x\longmapsto x^p\),
\(f(X)\) whose coefficients satisfy \(A_w^q=A_w\). Putting together (4.3.1) and (4.0.9), we find (taking into account the definition (4.1.0) of \(\chi\)):

We let \(\operatorname{Gal}(K/\mathbb Q_p(\zeta_p))\) act on \(K[[X_0,\ldots,X_{n+2}]]\) through its action on the coefficients; the component action of \(\sigma\in\operatorname{Gal}(K/\mathbb Q_p(\zeta_p))\) will be denoted
Taking into account that \(A_w^{\tau^j}=A_w^{p^j}\), (4.4.3) can then be rewritten
4.5. We shall now express the sum in (4.4.6) as a trace.
For each \(b\in\mathbb Q\), we define a \(p\)-adic Banach \(K\)-algebra
\[ \tag{4.5.1}L(b)=\text{the subring of }K[[X_0,\ldots,X_{n+2}]] \text{ consisting of the series} \]
It is a \(p\)-adic Banach algebra for the ordinal function (the negative of the logarithm of the norm)
One checks on (4.4.4) and (4.2.1) that we have
For each integer \(k>0\), we introduce a continuous operator of Banach spaces on \(K\)
For each pair \((b,b')\), \(b'<b\), there is a “restriction” operator which is completely continuous (i.e. a uniform limit of operators of finite rank)
Each element \(G\) of the algebra \(L(b)\) gives the continuous endomorphism of multiplication by \(G\)
These operators are related by the following lemma, whose proof can be found in [2] (cf. also [4]).

Lemma 4.5.8 (or the trace lemma (DWORK)).

Let \(b\in\mathbb Q\), \(b>0\), \(k\in\mathbb Z\), \(k\geq2\), and \(G\in L(b/k)\). Then the composite

\[ L(b)\xrightarrow{\text{« restriction »}}L(b/k) \xrightarrow{\times G}L(b/k)\xrightarrow{\psi_k}L(b), \]

denoted \(\psi_k\circ G\), is completely continuous, and its trace [cf. [4]] is given by the formula

\[ \tag{4.5.9}\operatorname{tr}(\psi_k\circ G) =\frac1{(k-1)^{n+2}}\sum_{X\in(\mu_{k-1}(\Omega))^{n+3}}G(X). \]
Let \(L^0(b)\) be the ideal of elements of \(L(b)\) which “vanish at 0”, i.e. “without constant term”. It is stable under \(\psi_k\circ G\), and we evidently have
Taking into account that \(H(0)=1\), (4.5.10) can be rewritten
One checks that its iterates are given by
and we note that, if \(\operatorname{ord}(q)\mid a\), we have \(\tau^{-a}=\operatorname{id}\). This granted, we define a \(\tau^{-1}\)-linear operation
For every subset \(A\subset S\), \(A\neq\varnothing\), we define a subspace of \(L(b)\):
\[ \tag{4.5.18}L^A(b)=\text{set of series }\sum B_UX^U\in L(b) \text{ such that, for each} \]
monomial \(X^U\) with \(B_U\neq0\), the \(U_i>0\) are exactly \(U_0\) and the \(U_j\), \(j\in A\).

Evidently each subspace \(\displaystyle\bigoplus_{A\supset B}L^A(b)\) is stable under \(\alpha\); it follows that the same is true for \(\displaystyle\bigoplus_{A\not\supset B}L^A(b)\), which is a sum of subspaces of the preceding type.

\[ \tag{4.5.21}\alpha_B=\text{the }\tau^{-1}\text{-linear endomorphism induced by }\alpha\text{ on} \] \[ \bigoplus_{A\supset B}L^A\left(\frac1{p-1}\right) \Big/\bigoplus_{\substack{A\supsetneqq B}}L^A\left(\frac1{p-1}\right) \simeq L^B\left(\frac1{p-1}\right). \]
where \(H_B(X)\) is deduced from \(H(X)\) by making the substitution \(X_i=0\) for \(i\notin\{0\}\cup B\).
Let us define, for \(A\subset S\), \(A\neq\varnothing\), the hypersurface
defined by \(f_A(X)\), the polynomial deduced from \(f(X)\) by making the substitution of \(X_i=0\) for \(i\notin A\) in it.

Taking into account the compatibility of the formation of the series \(H\) from \(f(X)\) with the operation of substituting 0 for some of the variables, (4.5.23) gives, for each \(A\subset S\), \(A\neq\varnothing\)
Note that \(\alpha\) hence, the \(\alpha_B\) being \(\tau^{-1}\) linear, the \(\alpha_B^{\operatorname{ord}(q)}\) are \(K\)-linear.
Let us then define the characteristic series
4.6. We shall now study the integrality of the series \(\Delta_B\). Let us set
and let us define a filtration on \(1+t\mathcal O[[t]]\) by
\[ \tag{4.6.3}\varphi(F^i)\subset F^{i+1}, \qquad \tag{4.6.4}\delta(F^i)\subset F^i, \] \[ \tag{4.6.5}\delta\text{ induces the identity on }F^i/F^{i+1}. \]

Lemma 4.6.6.

\(\Delta_B(t)\in1+t\mathcal O[[t]]\), and if an integer \(j\) satisfies

\[ \tag{4.6.7}jd<\operatorname{card}(B) \]

then we have

\[ \tag{4.6.8}\Delta_B(t)\in F^j(1+t\mathcal O[[t]]). \]
Proof. The Banach spaces \(L^B\left(\frac1{p-1}\right)\) are all “of type \(B(I)\)” in the sense of Serre [7], i.e. they admit “orthonormal bases”, namely, the monomials
which belong to them. That it is an “orthonormal” basis means by definition that we have
\[ \tag{4.6.11}R_B=\text{the }\mathcal O\text{-submodule of } L^B\left(\frac1{p-1}\right)\text{ consisting of the elements of ordinal }\geq0. \]
To prove the lemma, it suffices to prove

Let us verify (4.6.18) monomial by monomial. We have

\[ \tag{4.6.19}\left\{\begin{aligned} \psi_p(\pi^{U_0}X_0^{U_0}\cdots X_{n+2}^{U_{n+2}})&=0, &&\text{except if }\forall i\quad p\mid U_i,\\ \psi_p(\pi^{pU_0}X_0^{pU_0}\cdots X_{n+2}^{pU_{n+2}}) &=\pi^{(p-1)U_0}\cdot \underbrace{\pi^{U_0}X_0^{U_0}\cdots X_{n+2}^{U_{n+2}}}_{\in R_B}. \end{aligned}\right. \]
Now, the conditions for having \(X_0^{U_0}\cdots X_{n+2}^{U_{n+2}}\in L^B\left(\frac1{p-1}\right)\) are

pages.title: 5. A theorem of AX [1], (cf. also [4], section 7).

blocks.html: 5. A theorem of AX [1], (cf. also [4], section 7).

Theorem 5.0.

Let \(V\) be a hypersurface in \(mathbb P^{n+1}\) of degree \(d>0\), defined over \(mathbb F_q\), and let \(j\) be an integer: if we have

\[ \tag{5.0.1}jd<n+2, \]

then

\[ \tag{5.0.2}Z(t,V/\mathbb F_q)\prod_{i=0}^{n+1}(1-q^it) \in 1+q^jt\,\mathbb Z[[q^jt]], \]

and the inverse of every zero or pole of

\[ \tag{5.0.3}Z(t,V/\mathbb F_q)\prod_{i=0}^{n+1}(1-q^it) \]

is of the form (q^jcdot( ext{an algebraic integer})).

Theorem 5.0.bis.

Let \(V_{mathrm{aff}}\) be a hypersurface in \(mathbb A^{n+2}\) defined by a homogeneous form of degree \(d\), defined over \(mathbb F_q\). If we have

\[ \tag{5.0.7}jd<n+2 \]

then

\[ \tag{5.0.5}Z(t,V_{\mathrm{aff}}/\mathbb F_q)\in1+q^jt\,\mathbb Z[[q^jt]]. \]

In particular,

\[ \tag{5.0.6}\operatorname{card}(V_{\mathrm{aff}}(\mathbb F_q))\equiv0\pmod {q^j}, \]

and consequently

\[ \tag{5.0.6\,bis}\operatorname{card}(V(\mathbb F_q))\geq\frac{q^j-1}{q-1}>0 \]

(which improves the theorem of CHEVALLEY-WARNING [8]).

Proof. The implication (5.0.5) \(\Longrightarrow\) (5.0.6) is clear, by taking the coefficient of \(t\) in the zeta function. The assertions (5.0.4) and (5.0.2) are equivalent, taking into account that
The statements (5.0.2) and (5.0.3) are again equivalent, by the theorem of Fatou [3], thanks to the rationality of the zeta function. Since we have
Taking into account (4.5.33) and (4.6.4) – (4.6.5), it suffices to prove, for \(B\subset\{1,\ldots,n+2\}\), \(B\neq\varnothing\), that we have
We shall be able to finish the proof by applying the lemma (4.4.6), once the following assertion is proved:

Remark 5.1.

The condition

\[ \tag{5.1.1}jd<n+2 \]

admits a cohomological interpretation. Let \(V\) be a smooth hypersurface of degree \(d\) and of dimension \(n\). Set, as in XI 2.1, for \(a+b=n\)

\[ \tag{5.1.2}h_0^{a,b}=\dim H^b(V,\Omega_V^a)-\delta_{a,b} =\dim E^{a,b}(V) \]

where \(E^{a,b}(V)\) is the component of bidegree \((a,b)\) of the “vanishing Hodge cohomology” (compare XVII 5.4.4)

\[ \tag{5.1.3}E^{a,b}(V)=\operatorname{Coker} \left(H^b(\mathbb P^{n+1},\Omega_{\mathbb P^{n+1}}^a) \longrightarrow H^b(V,\Omega_V^a)\right). \]

Then, by XI, 2.8,

\[ \tag{5.1.4}jd<n+2\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad \left[h_0^{a,n-a}=0\text{ for }a<j\text{ and }a>n-j\right], \]

i.e. the condition means that the “Hodge level” (XI 2.7) of the “vanishing Hodge cohomology” of \(V\):

\[ E_{\mathrm{DR}}^n(V)=\operatorname{Coker} \left(H_{\mathrm{Hdg}}^n(\mathbb P^{n+1})\longrightarrow H_{\mathrm{Hdg}}^n(V)\right), \]

is (leq n-2j), i.e. its co-level is (geq j). On the other hand, the conclusion also means that the eigenvalues of the Frobenius \(mathcal F_q\) acting on the (ell)-adic vanishing cohomology \((\ell\neq p)\) are algebraic integers (XXI A.2) divisible by \(q^j\). The result of AX 5.0. thus comes in support of the following conjecture, suggested by the yoga of the level of motives, and which relates the classical conjectures of HODGE [5] and of TATE [5]:

Conjecture 5.2.

Let \(f:X\longrightarrow S\) be a proper and smooth morphism, with \(S\) integral, of finite type over \(operatorname{Spec}(\mathbb Z)\) and dominating \(operatorname{Spec}(\mathbb Z)\), and let \(i,j\) be integers. Suppose that the Hodge co-level of the \(H^i\) of the generic fiber of \(f\) is (geq j). Then for every point \(s\in S\), the co-level (XX 2.0.6) of \(H^i(X_s,\mathbb Q_\ell)\) is (geq j), hence (XX 2.2) if \(s\) is a closed point of \(S\), the eigenvalues of Frobenius acting on \(H^i(X_s,\mathbb Q_\ell)\) are algebraic integers divisible by \(q^j\), where \(q=\operatorname{card}(k(s))\).

pages.title: 6. End of the proof of the congruence formula.

blocks.html: 6. End of the proof of the congruence formula.
Taking into account (4.6.6) and (4.6.3)–(4.6.4), (4.5.24) gives a congruence formula
Let us pass to the computation of \(Delta_S(t)\) modulo \(\pi\,\mathcal O[[t]]\). Define (cf. (4.6.11)):

\[ \tag{6.0.3}\bar\alpha_S:W_S\longrightarrow W_S, \text{ the }\bar\tau^{-1}\text{-linear operation induced by }\alpha_S, \] \[ \tag{6.0.4}R_S(i)\text{ the submodule of }R_S\text{ consisting of the elements of degree }i\text{ in }X_0, \] \[ \tag{6.0.5}W_S(i)=R_S(i)\otimes_{\mathcal O}\mathbb F_q. \]

Proposition 6.0.6. We have the inclusion
\[ \alpha_S(R_S)\subset R_S(1)+pR_S. \]

Corollary 6.0.11. We have the inclusion
\[ \bar\alpha_S W_S\subset W_S(1). \]

Corollary 6.0.12. We have the congruence
\[ \Delta_S(t)\equiv\det(1-t(\bar\alpha_S)^{\operatorname{ord}(q)}\mid W_S(1)) \pmod {\pi\,\mathcal O[[t]]}. \]

Proposition 6.1. The \(\tau^{-1}\)-linear operation \(\bar\alpha_S:W_S(1)\longrightarrow W_S(1)\) is equal to the operation
\[ \tag{6.1.1}\tau^{-1}\frac1p\psi_p\circ(X_0f(X))^{p-1}. \]

\[ \begin{aligned} \tag{6.1.8}H(X) &=\prod_w\Phi(\widehat A_wX_0X^w)\\ &=\prod_w\exp\left(\sum_{n\geq0}\frac{(\pi\widehat A_wX_0X^w)^{p^n}}{p^n}\right)\\ &=\prod_w\prod_{n\geq0}\exp\left(\frac{(\pi\widehat A_wX_0X^w)^{p^n}}{p^n}\right)\\ &\equiv\prod_w\exp(\pi\widehat A_wX_0X^w)\pmod{\text{degree }p}\\ &\equiv\exp\left(\pi\sum_w\widehat A_wX_0X^w\right)\pmod{\text{degree }p}\\ &\equiv\exp(\pi X_0F(X))\pmod{\text{degree }p}, \end{aligned} \]

Corollary 6.1.12. The linear operator
\[ (\bar\alpha_S)^{\operatorname{ord}(q)}:W_S(1)\longrightarrow W_S(1) \]

is none other than \(\psi_q\circ(X_0f(X))^{q-1}\).

Corollary 6.1.13. We have the congruence mod \(p\):
\[ Z(t,V/\mathbb F_q)\equiv \frac{\det(1-t\psi_q(X_0f(X))^{q-1}\mid W_S(1))^{(-1)^{n+1}}}{1-t}. \]
It remains to compute the cohomology groups of \(\mathcal O_V\).
\[ \tag{6.2.1}\mathcal F_q=\text{the operation « raising to the }q\text{-th power » in }\mathcal O_V. \]
We have an exact sequence of sheaves on \(\mathbb P^{n+1}=\mathbb P\):

\[ \tag{6.2.3} \xymatrix{ 0\ar[r]&\mathcal O_{\mathbb P}(-d)\ar[r]^f\ar[d]_{f^{q-1}\mathcal F_q}& \mathcal O_{\mathbb P}\ar[r]\ar[d]_{\mathcal F_q}&\mathcal O_V\ar[r]\ar[d]_{\mathcal F_q}&0\\ 0\ar[r]&\mathcal O_{\mathbb P}(-d)\ar[r]^f&\mathcal O_{\mathbb P}\ar[r]&\mathcal O_V\ar[r]&0. } \]

\[ \tag{6.2.4}\left\{ \begin{aligned} H^0(\mathbb P^{n+1},\mathcal O_{\mathbb P})&\simeq\mathbb F_q,\\ H^i(\mathbb P^{n+1},\mathcal O_{\mathbb P})&=0&&i\neq0,\\ H^i(\mathbb P^{n+1},\mathcal O_{\mathbb P}(-d))&=0&&i\neq0,n+1, \end{aligned}\right. \]
the long exact cohomology sequence associated with (6.2.2) gives

\[ \tag{6.2.5}\left\{ \begin{aligned} H^0(V,\mathcal O_V)&\simeq\mathbb F_q,\\ H^i(V,\mathcal O_V)&=0&&\text{if }i\neq0,n,\\ H^n(V,\mathcal O_V)&\simeq H^{n+1}(\mathbb P^{n+1},\mathcal O_{\mathbb P}(-d)). \end{aligned}\right. \]
\[ \tag{6.2.7}\det(1-t\mathcal F_q\mid H^n(V,\mathcal O_V))= \det(1-t(f^{q-1}\mathcal F_q)\mid H^{n+1}(\mathbb P^{n+1},\mathcal O_{\mathbb P}(-d))). \]
\[ \tag{6.2.8}\det(1-t(f^{q-1}\mathcal F_q)\mid H^{n+1}(\mathbb P^{n+1},\mathcal O_{\mathbb P}(-d))) =\det(1-t(\psi_q\circ(X_0f(X))^{q-1}\mid W_S(1))) \]

Proposition 6.3. There is an isomorphism
\[ \tag{6.3.1}W_S(1)\simeq\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathbb F_q} (H^{n+1}(\mathbb P^{n+1},\mathcal O_{\mathbb P}(-d)),\mathbb F_q) \]

with respect to which the operator

\[ \tag{6.3.2}\psi_q\circ(X_0f(X))^{q-1}:W_S(1)\longrightarrow W_S(1) \]

is the transpose of

\[ \tag{6.3.3}f(X)^{q-1}\mathcal F_q:H^{n+1}(\mathbb P^{n+1},\mathcal O_{\mathbb P}(-d)) \longrightarrow H^{n+1}(\mathbb P^{n+1},\mathcal O_{\mathbb P}(-d)). \]
Proof. Computing the group \(H^{n+1}(\mathbb P^{n+1},\mathcal O_{\mathbb P}(-d))\) by means of the standard covering of \(\mathbb P^{n+1}\), we find

\[ \begin{aligned} \tag{6.3.4}H^{n+1}(\mathbb P^{n+1},\mathcal O_{\mathbb P}(-d)) &=\text{the }\mathbb F_q\text{-vector space with basis the monomials } X_1^{W_1}\cdots X_{n+2}^{W_{n+2}}\\ &\quad\text{the }W_i\in\mathbb Z\text{ satisfying }\sum W_i=-d\\ &=\text{the }\mathbb F_q\text{-subspace with basis the monomials } X_1^{W_1}\cdots X_{n+2}^{W_{n+2}}\\ &\quad\text{which moreover satisfy: }\nexists i\text{ such that }W_i\geq0. \end{aligned} \]
On the other hand, \(W_S(1)=R_S(1)\otimes_{\mathcal O}\mathbb F_q\), and we have
\[ \tag{6.3.5}R_S(1)=\text{the }\mathcal O\text{-module with basis the monomials } (\pi X_0)X_1^{U_1}\cdots X_{n+2}^{U_{n+2}} \text{ satisfying }\sum U_i=d,\quad U_i>0\ \forall i. \]

\[ \tag{6.3.7} (\operatorname{classe\ de}\ \pi X_0X_1^{U_1}\cdots X_{n+2}^{U_{n+2}}, X_1^{W_1}\cdots X_{n+2}^{W_{n+2}}) =\begin{cases}1&\text{if }U_i=-W_i\text{ for all }i,\\0&\text{otherwise} \end{cases} \]
is evidently a perfect duality of \(\mathbb F_q\)-vector spaces, and one easily checks that \(\psi_q\circ(X_0f(X))^{q-1}\) is the transpose of \(f(X)^{q-1}\mathcal F_q\), which proves 6.3.
